metadata.title: Prolonged fasting-induced metabolic signatures in human skeletal muscle of lean and obese men
metadata.study_design: Experimental study comparing lean and obese participants during fasting periods
metadata.demographics: 18 apparently healthy young men aged 20 to 35 years; nine lean (BMI 19-23) and nine obese (BMI 32-40 kg/m2)

evidence.quote: Prolonged fasting-induced metabolic signatures in human skeletal muscle of lean and obese men
18 apparently healthy young men aged from 20 to 35 years participated; nine lean (BMI 19–23) and nine obese (BMI 32–40 kg/m2)
we examined nine obese and nine lean participants during 12 and 72 h of fasting, respectively

methods_participants.label: Metabolomics Analysis
methods_participants.value: Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry

narrative_results.statement: Non-insulin-stimulated insulin levels were approximately 2–3 fold higher in obese than lean participants
Insulin levels decreased by at least 50% during 72 hour fasting in both groups
During hyperinsulinemic euglycemic clamp, insulin levels were approximately 15–30% higher in obese than lean participants
Prolonged fasting prompted a greater decrease in non-insulin-stimulated plasma glucose levels in lean (~35%) than obese (~25%)
Insulin-stimulation suppressed free fatty acid levels significantly in both groups, but less so during prolonged fasting
Non-insulin-stimulated plasma free fatty acid concentrations were higher in obese during an overnight fast, but lower during prolonged fasting

Despite previous observations of a higher lipolytic rate during 12 hour fasting in obese participants, increased free fatty acid content was not found in skeletal muscle from obese participants at any time point
Increased fat mass in obese was associated with higher levels of leptin and lower levels of adiponectin
Prolonged fasting reduced circulating leptin levels although at a higher level in obese participants

Despite an abundant supply of long-chain fatty acids from plasma during prolonged fasting, only modest increases in long chain fatty acids and no increases in acylcarnitines were found in skeletal muscle
Prolonged fasting increases triglyceride accumulation in skeletal muscle
Very high levels of β-OHB were found in fasting muscle with a concomitant increase in β-OHB-carnitine, demonstrating that ketone bodies were metabolized in skeletal muscle
There were no changes in NAD+, NADH, NADP+, FAD and AMP, ADP, ATP associated with prolonged fasting
Myocellular energetics were preserved and there were no suggestions of deficient substrate oxidation and obvious mitochondrial defects despite insulin resistance
In obese participants, the levels of TCA-cycle intermediates citrate, succinate, and malate were reduced compared to lean independently of the duration of the fast
Acetylcarnitine in obese was lower during 12 hour fasting, but normalized to levels similar to lean during 72 hour fasting
Endogenous glucose production was reduced during prolonged fasting
Glucose production in the non-insulin-stimulated state was lower in obese than lean during 12 hour fasting
In both obesity and fasting, insulin suppressed endogenous glucose production indicating that hepatic sensitivity to insulin was preserved
The capacity for glycogenesis in the obese, measured as insulin-stimulated net glucose disappearance during 12 hour fast, was reduced by approximately 45%
During prolonged fasting, glycogen levels in skeletal muscle were reduced approximately 20% in both groups
Glycogen synthase phosphorylation increased during prolonged fasting in obese similar to lean participants
Insulin stimulation during 72 hour fasting decreased glucose oxidation and glycogen synthase phosphorylation indicating that glucose incorporation into glycogen was prioritized
The metabolic adaptation to prolonged fasting involving changes in glycogen breakdown are lost in muscle from insulin resistant obese participants
The metabolic signatures in human skeletal muscle during acute and chronic insulin resistance reveal distinct differences

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass 1 reports 9 lean participants; Pass 2 reports 9 lean out of 18 total. Same value but different context/granularity suggests potential incompleteness in Pass 1
Pass 1 specifies two biopsy timings (60 and 270 minutes) while Pass 2 specifies only one (60 minutes). These are incompatible descriptions of the same measurement.
Pass_1 includes two time windows (180-210 and 300-330 min) while pass_2 only mentions 180-210 min; incomplete vs complete timing specification
Pass 1 describes insulin levels decreasing 50% during fasting; Pass 2 describes glycogen levels reducing ~20% in skeletal muscle. These are different metabolites and different physiological measures.
Pass 1 discusses FFA concentrations in obese during fasting states; Pass 2 discusses glucose production in non-insulin-stimulated state. Different metabolites and different comparisons.
Pass 1 addresses FFA content in skeletal muscle during fasting; Pass 2 addresses circulating leptin levels during prolonged fasting. Completely different measures and tissues.
Pass 1 describes leptin reduction during prolonged fasting; Pass 2 describes GS phosphorylation increase during prolonged fasting. Different biochemical measures.

Pass 1 discusses long-chain fatty acids and acylcarnitines in skeletal muscle during fasting. Pass 2 discusses NAD+, NADH, NADP+, FAD, and nucleotides. These are completely different metabolic parameters and cannot both be true descriptions of the same finding.

Pass 1 states myocellular energetics were preserved with no deficient substrate oxidation. Pass 2 states insulin stimulation decreased glucose oxidation. These represent conflicting conclusions about metabolic status.

Pass 1 discusses acetylcarnitine levels in obese during different fasting durations. Pass 2 discusses glycogenesis capacity and glycogen content. These are different metabolic measures and do not describe the same fact.

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Non-insulin-stimulated insulin levels were ~2–3 fold higher in obese than lean and decreased by at least 50% during 72h of fasting in both groups

semantic_groups.rationale: Same fasting duration for control group (12 hours); only labeling differs ('Control' vs '1')
Same fasting duration for intervention group (72 hours); only labeling differs ('Intervention' vs '2')
Pass 1 reports 9 lean participants; Pass 2 reports 9 lean out of 18 total. Same value but different context/granularity suggests potential incompleteness in Pass 1
Pass 1 reports obese participant count (9); Pass 2 reports lean group BMI range. These are different data types and cannot be compared
Same age inclusion criteria (20-35 years); minor formatting difference (hyphen vs 'to')
Pass 1 reports lean BMI criteria (19-23); Pass 2 reports obese BMI criteria (32-40). These are different groups and cannot be directly compared
Pass 1 contains obese BMI inclusion criteria (32-40); Pass 2 is empty/missing
Same physical activity inclusion criterion (low levels); only label formatting differs
Same fact: non-smokers as inclusion criterion. Only difference is 'Criteria' vs 'Criterion' (plural vs singular).
Same fact: no prescription medications as inclusion criterion. Only difference is 'Criteria' vs 'Criterion' (plural vs singular).
Pass 1 specifies two biopsy timings (60 and 270 minutes) while Pass 2 specifies only one (60 minutes). These are incompatible descriptions of the same measurement.
Same fact: muscle biopsy size of 100 mg. Pass 2 adds 'approximately' which is a minor qualifier that doesn't contradict the value.
Same fact: vastus lateralis of quadriceps femoris as biopsy location. Only difference is capitalization of 'Vastus' vs 'vastus'.
Same fact: 2-hour duration of hyperinsulinemic euglycemic clamp. Only difference is abbreviation expansion (HEC vs full name).
Same fact: 30 mU insulin/m2/min infusion rate. Only difference is abbreviation expansion (HEC vs full name).
Pass 1 contains glucose target level information (5 mM) but Pass 2 is empty. This is an orphan entry from Pass 1.
Both describe DXA for body composition; pass_1 specifies QDR-2000 model while pass_2 uses generic DXA acronym
Pass_1 includes two time windows (180-210 and 300-330 min) while pass_2 only mentions 180-210 min; incomplete vs complete timing specification
Both describe YSI 2300 STAT Plus glucose analyzer; minor wording differences in labeling
Both describe ELISA for insulin assay; pass_1 specifies manufacturer (DAKO, Agilent) while pass_2 only repeats ELISA
C-peptide assay only in pass_1; no corresponding entry in pass_2
Cortisol assay only in pass_1; no corresponding entry in pass_2
Glucagon assay only in pass_1; no corresponding entry in pass_2
Free fatty acids assay only in pass_1; no corresponding entry in pass_2
Pass 1 contains glycerol assay information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains beta-hydroxybutyrate assay information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains metabolomics analysis information; Pass 2 is empty
Both describe identical randomized crossover design
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains muscle biopsy timing information
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains indirect calorimetry timing information
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains HbA1c measurement assay information
Pass 1 describes insulin levels decreasing 50% during fasting; Pass 2 describes glycogen levels reducing ~20% in skeletal muscle. These are different metabolites and different physiological measures.
Both describe the same finding: insulin levels ~15-30% higher in obese vs lean during hyperinsulinemic euglycemic clamp. Minor abbreviation difference (HEC vs full name).
Identical statements describing plasma glucose decrease during prolonged fasting in lean (~35%) vs obese (~25%).

Both describe FFA suppression by insulin in both groups, less during prolonged fasting. Pass 2 adds quantitative detail (~3-4 fold higher FFA comparing 72h vs 12h fasting) not in Pass 1, but core finding is the same.
Pass 1 discusses FFA concentrations in obese during fasting states; Pass 2 discusses glucose production in non-insulin-stimulated state. Different metabolites and different comparisons.
Pass 1 addresses FFA content in skeletal muscle during fasting; Pass 2 addresses circulating leptin levels during prolonged fasting. Completely different measures and tissues.

Both describe the same relationship: increased fat mass in obese associated with higher leptin and lower adiponectin. Pass 2 adds interpretive context about insulin resistance but the core fact is identical.
Pass 1 describes leptin reduction during prolonged fasting; Pass 2 describes GS phosphorylation increase during prolonged fasting. Different biochemical measures.

Pass 1 discusses long-chain fatty acids and acylcarnitines in skeletal muscle during fasting. Pass 2 discusses NAD+, NADH, NADP+, FAD, and nucleotides. These are completely different metabolic parameters and cannot both be true descriptions of the same finding.
Pass 1 contains a statement about triglyceride accumulation; Pass 2 is empty. This is an orphan entry from Pass 1.
Pass 1 contains a statement about β-OHB and ketone body metabolism; Pass 2 is empty. This is an orphan entry from Pass 1.
Pass 1 contains a statement about NAD+, NADH, NADP+, FAD, and nucleotides; Pass 2 is empty. This is an orphan entry from Pass 1.

Pass 1 states myocellular energetics were preserved with no deficient substrate oxidation. Pass 2 states insulin stimulation decreased glucose oxidation. These represent conflicting conclusions about metabolic status.

Both passages describe the same finding: reduced TCA-cycle intermediates (citrate, succinate, malate) in obese vs lean participants independent of fasting duration. Wording is nearly identical with only minor stylistic differences.

Pass 1 discusses acetylcarnitine levels in obese during different fasting durations. Pass 2 discusses glycogenesis capacity and glycogen content. These are different metabolic measures and do not describe the same fact.
Pass 1 contains a statement about endogenous glucose production during prolonged fasting; Pass 2 is empty. This is an orphan entry from Pass 1.
Pass 1 contains a specific finding about glucose production in fasting; Pass 2 is empty
Both describe insulin suppression of EGP and preserved hepatic insulin sensitivity in obesity and fasting. Pass 2 adds contextual detail about muscle contrast but conveys the same core finding
Pass 1 contains specific finding about glycogenesis capacity reduction in obese; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains finding about glycogen reduction during prolonged fasting; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains finding about glycogen synthase phosphorylation; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains finding about insulin stimulation effects during 72 hour fasting; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains finding about metabolic adaptation loss in obese participants; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains general statement about metabolic signatures; Pass 2 is empty

Both describe the same core finding about non-insulin-stimulated insulin levels being 2-3 fold higher in obese vs lean participants. Pass_2 adds additional information about fasting effects, but the overlapping portion is equivalent with only minor notation differences (~vs approximately).
